common_name: Yellow Oleander (Lucky Nut, Be-Still Tree, Cascabela thevetia)

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in full sun with well-drained soil; Yellow Oleander grows rapidly in zone 9-10 and begins blooming in the first year. In zone 9a, it may be damaged by hard frost but resproots from the root crown in spring. In zone 9b-10, it grows as a reliable evergreen shrub blooming nearly year-round. Prune after each bloom flush to shape and stimulate reblooming. The milky sap is a severe skin and eye irritant. EXTREME CAUTION: All parts contain highly toxic cardiac glycosides — seeds especially dangerous and have caused accidental poisoning fatalities worldwide.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy to moderately fertile soil; Yellow Oleander is drought-tolerant once established and will rot in heavy, consistently wet soils. Cascabela thevetia (formerly Thevetia peruviana) is a large, evergreen shrub or small tree from Mexico, Central America, and the Caribbean in the Apocynaceae family — distantly related to Oleander (Nerium) and sharing its extreme toxicity. The plant produces long, narrow, deep green, glossy, lance-shaped leaves that densely clothe the arching stems, and bears funnel-shaped, slightly fragrant flowers 2–3 inches across in brilliant yellow — occasionally orange-yellow or peach in some forms. Flowering occurs year-round in zone 10-11 and from spring through fall in zone 9, with peak bloom in warm months. In tropical and subtropical regions worldwide, Yellow Oleander is one of the most commonly planted ornamental shrubs for hot, dry, full-sun landscapes — its combination of attractive foliage, continuous flowers, and extreme drought and heat tolerance makes it exceptionally useful. EXTREME CAUTION: All parts of Yellow Oleander contain highly toxic cardiac glycosides (thevetin A and B, neriifolin) that affect the heart — the large black seeds are particularly concentrated and have caused accidental poisonings and intentional toxicity events worldwide; do not plant where children, pets, or livestock have access.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cascabela thevetia (Yellow Oleander)
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Thevetia peruviana for Gulf Coast zone 9-10
LSU AgCenter — Tropical shrubs for Gulf Coast Louisiana
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Cascabela thevetia (highly toxic)

common_name: Hardy Elephant Ear (New Guinea Shield, Purple-Back Elephant Ear)

planting.note: Plant rhizomes or container-grown specimens in spring in partial shade with moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; Alocasia wentii is one of the more cold-tolerant Alocasias, surviving temperatures to 25–28°F with heavy mulch protection in zone 8b. In zone 9, it grows as a reliable perennial — the rhizomes overwinter underground and resprout in spring. Provide consistently moist soil for best foliage; will go partially dormant in dry conditions. Propagate by dividing rhizome offsets. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant in partial shade to dappled light in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil; Alocasia wentii performs best in the kind of consistently moist, partly shaded conditions found under large trees with moist, humus-rich soil — conditions that approximate its native humid forest habitat. Alocasia wentii is a rhizomatous tropical aroid native to New Guinea in the family Araceae, valued in zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens for two qualities that distinguish it from most Alocasias: its relatively greater cold tolerance and its unusually attractive bi-colored leaves. The large, arrowhead-shaped to cordate (heart-shaped) leaves are medium to dark green with a slight bronzy sheen on the upper surface and a distinctive, rich purple-bronze to mahogany-purple on the underside — the purple undersides are revealed whenever the wind moves the leaves, creating a bi-color effect that is particularly striking in a mass planting. The plant produces the modest, spathe-and-spadix flowers typical of the aroid family, but these are secondary to the foliage display. As one of the hardier Alocasia species, A. wentii has become a more reliable choice than the tender A. odora and other commonly grown Alocasias for zone 8-9 gardens where temperatures occasionally drop below freezing. Established clumps divide and spread to form attractive colonies. All parts toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Alocasia species for Gulf Coast Florida
LSU AgCenter — Cold-Hardy Elephant Ears for Louisiana Zone 8-9
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Alocasia wentii for Gulf Coast zone 8-9
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Alocasia (toxic to pets)

common_name: Dancing Lady Orchid (Golden Shower Orchid, Tiger Orchid, Sphacelatum)

planting.note: Grow in a slatted pot or basket with coarse bark media in bright indirect to filtered direct light; Oncidium sphacelatum is one of the more tolerant Oncidiums, adapting to a wider range of conditions than many spray orchids. In zone 9, it can grow on a shaded porch in a hanging basket from spring through fall, then moved indoors in winter. The plant blooms from pseudobulbs of the previous season — maintain 3+ mature pseudobulbs for reliable blooming. Water regularly while in growth, reduce in winter rest. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to gentle direct morning sun — Dancing Lady Orchid produces its best blooms with 4–6 hours of bright filtered light and will not bloom reliably in dim indoor conditions. Oncidium sphacelatum is a large, clump-forming, sympodial epiphytic orchid native to Mexico, Guatemala, Honduras, El Salvador, Belize, Venezuela, and Colombia, widely distributed in highland tropical forests from sea level to 5,000+ feet. It is one of the largest commonly grown Oncidiums and produces some of the most spectacular spray-type orchid inflorescences available: long, arching, multi-branched panicles 3–5 feet in length carrying hundreds of individual flowers in vivid yellow and chestnut-brown — the small (half-inch to 1-inch) flowers have brown-barred petals and a dominant, bright yellow, flared lip that gives dancing lady orchids their name (the yellow lip resembles the skirt of a dancer twirling in a yellow dress with brown accents). A well-established plant in full bloom with 2–3 long, branching inflorescences simultaneously open is one of the most dramatic of all orchid displays. The plant is a prolific grower that divides readily, forming large clumps. Oncidium sphacelatum has been used extensively in producing the Oncidium intergeneric hybrids now widely sold as potted orchids. Non-toxic to pets.
sources: American Orchid Society — Oncidium sphacelatum cultivation guide
Royal Horticultural Society — Oncidium cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Oncidium orchids for indoor culture
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Oncidium (non-toxic)

common_name: Parakeet Heliconia (Lobster Claw Heliconia, Tan Heliconia, Expanded Heliconia)

planting.note: Plant rhizomes or container-grown clumps in spring when soil temperature reaches 65°F+ in partial to full shade with consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; Heliconia latispatha is one of the most adaptable and widely grown heliconias for zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens, tolerating moderate drought once established better than many species. In zone 9, the rhizomes survive mild winters underground with heavy mulch protection; in zone 9b-10, it grows as a reliable perennial clump. Divide and spread clumps every 3–4 years. Excellent as a tropical cut flower with 2–3 week vase life.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil; Parakeet Heliconia grows well in partial shade and can tolerate more direct sun than many heliconias, making it more versatile in zone 9 garden conditions. Heliconia latispatha is a clump-forming, rhizomatous tropical perennial native to an extremely wide range from Mexico through Central America and into Ecuador, Colombia, and Peru in the Heliconiaceae family — one of the most geographically widespread of the heliconias, which explains its adaptability to diverse growing conditions. The plant produces the characteristic banana-like paddle-shaped leaves on tall upright pseudostems and, in the warm months, erect inflorescences with boat-shaped bracts in yellow-orange to orange-red with vivid green tips (in the most common form), arranged alternately along the stem in a 'lobster claw' pattern. Multiple color forms exist, ranging from purely yellow to orange to red, with various tip and margin colorations. The flowers within the bracts are small and white, pollinated by hummingbirds attracted to the brightly colored bracts. Heliconia latispatha is considered one of the best heliconias for zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens because of its relative cold tolerance, adaptability to partial shade, and consistent blooming performance through the warm months. The inflorescences are long-lasting cut flowers.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Heliconia latispatha for Florida gardens
LSU AgCenter — Heliconia species for Gulf Coast Louisiana
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Heliconia latispatha cultivars
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Heliconia for Gulf Coast zone 9 tropical gardens

planting.note: Plant rhizomes or container-grown clumps in spring in partial shade to full sun with consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Heliconia wagneriana is one of the more cold-sensitive heliconias and requires consistently warm conditions — in zone 9a, protect rhizomes with heavy mulch in winter and expect top growth to die back. In zone 9b-10, it grows as a large, reliable perennial with spectacular spring bloom coinciding with Easter. The large inflorescences are among the most prized in the heliconia family for cut flower use — long vase life of 3–4 weeks.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil — Easter Heliconia grows most vigorously and produces its largest inflorescences with morning sun and afternoon shade protection in zone 9-10's intense climate. Heliconia wagneriana is a large, clump-forming, rhizomatous tropical perennial native to Costa Rica, Panama, Colombia, and Ecuador in the Heliconiaceae family, named for an early botanical collector and commonly called 'Easter Heliconia' because its natural blooming peak in the Northern Hemisphere typically coincides with the Easter season (March–April). The plant produces large, erect inflorescences (flower spikes) 24–36 inches long with 8–12 alternating, boat-shaped bracts in a spectacular color combination: the bracts are bright red to deep orange-red with bright yellow edges and green-tipped margins, giving the inflorescence a multi-colored, rainbow-like appearance that is unique among commonly grown heliconias. The precisely alternating arrangement of the bracts, each perfectly clasping the next, creates an almost geometric, architectural inflorescence. Heliconia wagneriana is highly valued in the cut flower industry for its extraordinary inflorescences, which have vase lives of 3–4 weeks when properly conditioned. In zone 10-11 Gulf Coast and South Florida tropical gardens, established clumps produce multiple inflorescences per season and become impressive landscape features.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Heliconia wagneriana for Florida landscapes
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Heliconia wagneriana cut flower notes
Heliconia Society International — Heliconia wagneriana species profile
LSU AgCenter — Tropical Ornamentals for Gulf Coast Louisiana Zone 10
